result:
There’s a kind of silence that isn’t empty but humming—like the static before a rainfall, or the moment before a door you didn’t know existed swings open. I walked into that silence on a Tuesday afternoon when the sun was pretending not to care and the shop door chimed like it knew my middle name. The antique clock repair place smelled of brass filings and cedar; gears lay on black velvet like miniature planets waiting to be threaded into orbits they’d forgotten. One clock on the wall ran backward. I pointed. The owner shrugged. “Some stories insist on returning to their beginnings.”

I bought the backward clock for the price of a half-moon croissant and three years of insomnia. On the walk home, every sparrow I passed flew in reverse; leaves unrustled, the wind sucked instead of blew. I started laughing and couldn’t stop because none of it was funny. Laughter, too, wanted to back into the throat it had come from.

At night the house tick-tocked in the wrong direction. Shadows re-collected their owner so neatly that by 3 a.m. I stood precisely where I’d been at 5 p.m. the previous day, hand frozen mid-reach toward the kettle. Then yesterday began again—the kettle boiled, the phone pinged, the cat tripped me, and I cursed in chronological order until time noticed me frowning and let December slip into November like stepping stones across a river.

I wrote a letter to my mother before she died. The paper stayed blank until 2 October rolled around, when every attempted apology—some harsh, most laced with guilt the way lace is laced with holes—rose out of the ink and dissolved back into the nib. The pen sucked punctuation like string through a keyhole; it left me clutching possibility instead of sentences. She’d never read the letter now, but I tucked it inside the clock, behind the pendulum that swung the opposite of forth. Retrace a grief far enough and you end up holding the embryo of a future.

I started meeting people I was going to meet tomorrow. There was a café where the barista hadn’t yet burned the milk I would complain about in fourteen hours. Already angry, I ordered a decaf; steam wormed its way into the pitcher like salmon swimming home. We talked backwards: “Glad weren’t you? Told I as you, see.” Her eyes, liquid brown, said normal things anyway—have a good day, try the rye next time—because kindness isn’t bound to tense. I left a tip gleaming on the counter she’d already cleaned.

One day I met a woman at the fountain she would someday throw coins into for wishes that hadn’t yet learned the shape of longing. She sat reading yesterday’s paper, tomorrow’s obituary with somebody else’s name in it. When our eyes linked, the swooping numbness that usually arrives after an ache arrived before. We both said “Hello” at the end of the conversation, and it sounded like “Goodbye” spoken inside out. We walked the same sidewalk opposite each other for hours, growing farther apart until we collided at a bench neither remembered sitting down on yet. She had freckles shaped like a constellation I used to count when I was six and fearless of darkness. That constellation, according to the sky, wouldn’t be visible until next spring, but it was visible on her clavicle now. I gave her my name. She forgot it forward.

Days blurred into fevers of calendar pages flipping downward. I learned to tie shoes by untying them until my fingers grasped the start of laces. I lost scars by watching them stitch closed. My plants wilted back into seed packets. The cat grew younger, tinier, until she mewed like a postcard I meant to send myself from a city I’d once left. Every evening light crept back behind the horizon until the sky, embarrassed, forgot how to blush. There was comfort in the rewind, like petting velvet against the grain; ridges rose under the hand’s backward direction and felt undiscovered.

Eventually I stood at the threshold of the antique shop again but hadn’t yet stepped inside. The owner was younger, beard short, eyes stitched with different kinds of doubt. The backward clock hung on the wall ticking forward. I stared at it until the sound became a question neither of us could pronounce. “Would you like to buy it?” he asked, younger. “Not yet,” I answered. I left before leaving arrived.

Outside the sky finally learned how to blush. On the opposite sidewalk the woman walked away from me, shoulder blades sliding beneath a jacket the color of dawn. She turned once, waved a farewell that felt like hello aging gracefully. I wanted to call after her but my throat contained only the inhale before a never-uttered name. Instead I watched her disappear backward into the rush hour she still belonged to, then forward into whatever came before. 

When I reached the park the fountain had coins glistening on the bottom they would someday leave fingers to leap back into. I sat on the edge. Between my palms the embryo of a future shifted—a small ticking sound, like an unborn heart. I opened my hand. There was the letter I’d never finished writing to my mother, but now it started “Dear Child-Who-Will-Speak-This.” The page bore tomorrow’s apology in ink wet as promise: Mum, I’m sorry I never thanked you for teaching me how to turn loss around in my hands until it glows like warm brass. For showing me every ending is merely the underside of a beginning waking up.

I folded the letter small enough to slip between the mint-fresh coins. Immediately it bloomed into a paper boat that fetched itself onto the surface tension instead of sinking. It sailed in tight circles, collecting seconds, reversing course when the wind that hadn’t blown yet caught its miniature sail. Then it lifted—light as a word swallowed before hurt—and flew straight into the sun now rising backward. The light that had swallowed the letter burst into embers that became sparrows, baby-sparrows, fledging backwards into eggs then into potential. Before any of that existed, I watched, quietly, until the present—tender, impossible—balanced on the lip of my tongue like a comma about to become the rest of the sentence.

At 5 p.m. on a day the backward clock now promised was coming, I opened my front door. The cat meowed new and uncynical and leapt into my arms. The kettle had never cooled; the phone had never buzzed. I set the cat down, boiled fresh water, made tea the color of amber before amber. Steam curled into a shape that looked like tomorrow arriving sideways. I took the first sip and tasted beginnings, bitter, hot, true.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
